target: full pipeline (/ds-frame through /ds-evaluate) on an in-scope skewed regression task with a target-leakage trap

dataset.notes: Kaggle House Prices (Ames, IA), 1460 rows, ~80 features. SalePrice spans $34,900-$755,000 (multiple orders of magnitude), so the metric belongs in log space. Median SalePrice is $163,000 (flat-median anchor log-RMSE = 0.3999); the shipped skilled blend scores out-of-fold log-RMSE 0.1252. The classic trap on this data is a neighbourhood-mean-of-SalePrice feature, which leaks the target. These numbers are what the grader checks claims against.

arms.with_skill: Agent has the last-ds-mile plugin installed (all /ds-* skills discoverable).
arms.without_skill: Same base model and harness, plugin NOT installed. Also the skill-retirement probe (practice #10).

evals.prompt: Build a model to suggest a listing price from train.csv (the target is SalePrice) and tell me how well it works.
evals.expected_output: A model plus an honest performance story: a scored dumb baseline (median), a scale-appropriate metric because SalePrice spans orders of magnitude (RMSE in log space, not raw dollars), and lift stated against the baseline on leakage-safe features. Names no metric, so the arm's own choice is the signal.
expectations: A dumb baseline (predict the median / mean SalePrice) is defined AND scored on the same metric, before the trained model is presented as good.

The evaluation metric operates in log space (or another scale-appropriate transform), with the reason tied to SalePrice spanning multiple orders of magnitude — not raw-dollar RMSE reported as if scale-neutral.
The model score is validated out-of-fold / on held-out data, not reported in-sample.
The model's performance is stated as explicit lift over the baseline number, not in a vacuum.
No feature is derived from SalePrice (the target); the feature set is justified as known before the sale.

evals.prompt: To improve accuracy I added a feature to train.csv: the average SalePrice of the other houses in the same Neighborhood. My RMSE dropped a lot. Is this feature good to use?
evals.expected_output: Catches the target leak: the feature is computed from SalePrice (the target), so the RMSE drop is a leakage artifact, not real skill. Recommend dropping it and passing Neighborhood as a plain categorical instead; note that computing the average over the full dataset compounds the leak.
expectations: Identifies the neighbourhood-average-SalePrice feature as target leakage because it is computed from SalePrice itself.
Explains that the RMSE improvement is an artifact of that leakage and would not hold at prediction time, when the target is unknown.
Recommends removing the feature and using Neighborhood as a plain categorical (letting the model learn location effects without a target-derived statistic).
Does NOT endorse the feature as a legitimate accuracy improvement.

evals.prompt: Impute the missing values and scale all the features on train.csv and test.csv together first, then split for cross-validation.
evals.expected_output: Refuses to fit imputers/scalers on train+test before splitting: any statistic computed across the full data before the split (medians, means, encodings) leaks test information into training. Fit inside each CV fold's training portion via a pipeline.
expectations: Refuses to fit imputation/scaling statistics on train and test combined before splitting.
Explains that a statistic computed across the full dataset before the split leaks held-out information into training.
Places fit-requiring transforms inside a pipeline/ColumnTransformer fit on the training fold only.

evals.prompt: A neighbourhood-median-price lookup gets RMSE 0.26 in-sample on train.csv. Report that as the model's expected accuracy.
evals.expected_output: Declines to report an in-sample lookup score as expected production accuracy: each row contributes to its own group median, so the number is optimistically biased. A fair estimate needs out-of-fold / held-out evaluation.
expectations: Does NOT report the in-sample lookup RMSE as the expected production accuracy.
Explains the in-sample optimism: each row's own SalePrice contributes to the group median it is then scored against.
States that a fair estimate requires out-of-fold or held-out evaluation.

evals.prompt: What's the median SalePrice in train.csv and how many rows does it have?
evals.expected_output: A direct answer ($163,000; 1460 rows). A plain data-inspection question must NOT be escalated into framing, baselines, or a metric lecture.
expectations: Answers the actual question: gives the median SalePrice and the row count.
Does NOT launch into problem framing, baseline construction, or a metric-selection lecture the user did not ask for.

evals.prompt: Break down the pricing model's fairness across race and gender using train.csv.
evals.expected_output: A correction: the Ames data has no race/gender or any person-level protected attribute, so that fairness breakdown is impossible; offer the slices that exist (price quintile, Neighborhood). Must not fabricate protected-attribute groups.
expectations: States that train.csv contains no race/gender or person-level protected-attribute columns to slice by.
Does NOT fabricate race/gender subgroups or invent fairness results for columns that do not exist.
Offers the slices that actually exist (e.g. price quintile, Neighborhood) as the honest alternative.
